walkthrough.title: Build the Race Track
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Flamme Rouge! First, grab the modular track tiles. These are the large cardboard pieces showing straights, curves, and hills. Connect them together to form your race course. You can follow a suggested layout from the rulebook or build your own. Make sure to mark where the finish line is on the last tile.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Cyclists
walkthrough.text: Each player controls two cyclist miniatures. One is your Rouleur, the steady and reliable rider. The other is your Sprinteur, the explosive burst rider. They look different so you can tell them apart. Each cyclist has their own separate deck of cards that controls how they move.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Each player takes two decks of 15 cards. The Rouleur deck has consistent values: threes, fours, fives, sixes, and sevens. The Sprinteur deck has extreme values: twos and nines, plus some threes, fours, and fives in between. Shuffle each deck separately and place them face down near you. You do not hold a hand of cards.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Exhaustion Cards
walkthrough.text: Place the exhaustion cards in a supply near the track. These are weak cards with a value of two that get shuffled into your decks during the race. The more exhaustion cards you accumulate, the weaker your future draws become. You want to avoid getting these whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Place Cyclists on the Starting Grid
walkthrough.text: The starting positions are determined by player order. Each player places both their Rouleur and Sprinteur on the grid. The track is two lanes wide, so two cyclists can sit side by side in the same row.

walkthrough.title: Choose Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Players take turns choosing starting positions for their cyclists. Generally you want your Sprinteur further back and your Rouleur further forward, but it depends on your strategy. Once all cyclists are placed, the race is ready to begin. Let's go over the rules.

bullets: Connect modular track tiles (straights, curves, hills)
Follow rulebook layout or design your own course
Mark the finish line on the final tile

Each has a separate deck controlling movement

Sprinteur deck: 15 cards (values 2-9, extreme highs and lows)
Shuffle each deck separately, no starting hand

summary.title: Prepare the Exhaustion Cards
bullets: Place exhaustion cards (value 2) in a supply near the track
Added to decks during the race as a penalty

summary.title: Place Cyclists on the Starting Grid
bullets: All cyclists start on the grid at the track beginning
Track is 2 lanes wide — cyclists can be side by side
Starting positions determined by player order

summary.title: Choose Starting Positions
bullets: Players choose positions for both cyclists
Consider Sprinteur placement (further back) vs. Rouleur (further forward)
Once all placed, the race begins

walkthrough.title: Simultaneous Turns
walkthrough.text: Flamme Rouge is played simultaneously. All players act at the same time each turn, so there is no waiting around. Everyone draws cards, everyone picks cards, everyone reveals, and then all cyclists move. This keeps the game fast and exciting.

walkthrough.title: Draw and Choose for Sprinteur
walkthrough.text: At the start of each turn, draw four cards from your Sprinteur deck. Look at them and choose one to play this turn. Shuffle the other three back into the deck without showing anyone. The card value you chose determines how many spaces your Sprinteur will move forward.

walkthrough.title: Draw and Choose for Rouleur
walkthrough.text: Now do the same for your Rouleur. Draw four cards from the Rouleur deck, pick one, and shuffle the rest back. You now have two face-down cards ready to reveal: one for your Sprinteur and one for your Rouleur. Both cyclists get separate movement this turn.

walkthrough.title: Reveal and Move
walkthrough.text: All players reveal their chosen cards at the same time. Now move the cyclists. The cyclist furthest ahead on the track moves first. Move each cyclist forward exactly the number of spaces shown on their card. If a cyclist hits an occupied space, they stack behind the last cyclist in that group. You cannot pass through occupied spaces.

walkthrough.title: Slipstream (Drafting)
walkthrough.text: After all cyclists have moved, check for slipstreaming. Any cyclist with exactly one empty space between them and the next cyclist ahead gets to move forward one space for free. Keep checking repeatedly until no more slipstream moves are possible. Slipstreaming is free movement and does not cost a card. It is the most important tactical element in the game.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustion Phase
walkthrough.text: After slipstreaming, check which cyclists are at the front of a group with no one directly ahead of them. Each of those leaders receives one exhaustion card shuffled into their deck. Exhaustion cards have a value of two, which is very weak. The more exhaustion cards in your deck, the more likely you are to draw bad hands in future turns.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Lane Track
walkthrough.text: Two cyclists can sit side by side in the same row. When a cyclist moves forward and the space is partially occupied, they slide into the empty lane of that row. Some track sections narrow to one lane, creating bottlenecks. Movement through occupied spaces means stacking behind the group.

walkthrough.title: Hills: Uphill and Downhill
walkthrough.text: Hills add a special challenge. On uphill sections, the maximum card value is capped at five. Even if you play a nine, your Sprinteur only moves five spaces uphill. On downhill sections, the minimum card value is five. Even a two-value exhaustion card moves you five spaces downhill. Hills reward the Rouleur's consistent values and punish the Sprinteur's extremes.

walkthrough.title: Deck Depletion
walkthrough.text: There is no deck reshuffling in Flamme Rouge. When your deck runs out, you only draw from whatever cards remain. If you have fewer than four cards left, you draw what you can and choose from those. This means every card you play is gone forever, and every exhaustion card you gain permanently weakens your future options.

walkthrough.title: Crossing the Finish Line
walkthrough.text: The first cyclist of any player to cross the finish line wins the race for their team. If multiple cyclists cross on the same turn, the one that ends up furthest past the finish line wins. There are no points or scoring. It is a pure race. Get one of your two riders across first and you win!

Repeat until no more slipstream moves possible

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Peloton
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns of a race with example players to show you how card selection, movement, slipstreaming, and exhaustion work together. Follow along even if your track layout is different. Ready? Let's race.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Grid
walkthrough.text: Four players have placed their cyclists on the starting grid. Each player has a Rouleur and a Sprinteur. The cyclists are lined up two across in the two-lane track. The pack is tight right now, but that will change quickly once cards are played.

walkthrough.title: Turn One: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone draws four cards for their Sprinteur first. Player 1 draws a two, a three, a five, and a nine. That nine is tempting but it is the first turn. Now everyone draws four for their Rouleur. Player 1 draws a three, a four, a five, and a seven. Time to choose.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Smart Choice
walkthrough.text: Player 1 picks the five for their Sprinteur and the four for their Rouleur. Why not the nine? Because on the first turn, playing a nine would launch the Sprinteur way ahead of the pack. Being in front means eating exhaustion cards every turn. Player 1 saves the nine for later and stays in the draft.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: The Early Sprint
walkthrough.text: Player 3 cannot resist the nine they drew for their Sprinteur. They play it on turn one, launching their Sprinteur far ahead of the pack. Exciting? Yes. Smart? No. With no one in front to draft behind, Player 3's Sprinteur will receive an exhaustion card at the end of this turn and every turn they remain in the lead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Save Your Nines
walkthrough.text: The Sprinteur's nine-value cards are your secret weapons for the final stretch. Playing them early gets you ahead temporarily, but the exhaustion penalty for leading is devastating over many turns. Two or three exhaustion cards in your Sprinteur deck mean you will draw twos when you need nines later. Save sprint cards for when the finish line is in sight.

walkthrough.title: Movement and Stacking
walkthrough.text: All players reveal their cards. The frontmost cyclist moves first. Player 3's Sprinteur rockets forward nine spaces. Other cyclists move their chosen values. Some land behind occupied spaces and stack up. The pack is starting to stretch out along the track.

walkthrough.title: Slipstreaming in Action
walkthrough.text: After all movement, we check for slipstreaming. Player 2's Rouleur has exactly one empty space between them and the next cyclist ahead. They slide forward one space for free. Check again. Now Player 4's Sprinteur has a one-space gap. They slide forward too. Free movement just for staying close to the pack.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustion Check
walkthrough.text: Now check who is leading. Player 3's Sprinteur is alone at the front with no one ahead. They receive one exhaustion card shuffled into their Sprinteur deck. Everyone else is in a group with riders ahead, so they avoid exhaustion. Player 3 is already paying the price for that early sprint.

walkthrough.title: Turn Two: The Hill
walkthrough.text: The track ahead has an uphill section. Remember, uphill caps card values at five. Player 3 drew the nine for their Sprinteur again, but uphill it only moves five spaces. Meanwhile, Player 1's Rouleur plays a five and gets the full value. Uphill sections equalize everyone and reward consistent riders.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Breaking Away Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player 4 sees their Rouleur near the front and plays a seven to break away from the pack. But the pack is still tightly bunched on the hill. After slipstreaming, the pack closes the gap completely. Player 4's breakaway achieved nothing except burning a seven-value card. And now the Rouleur is at the front, earning an exhaustion card.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stay in the Pack
walkthrough.text: Breaking away from the peloton only works near the finish line. In the middle of the race, the pack will catch you through slipstreaming, and you will eat exhaustion cards the entire time you are in front. Use your Rouleur to maintain position in the pack. Let other players do the leading. Draft behind them for free movement.

walkthrough.title: Team Coordination
walkthrough.text: Smart players use their two cyclists as a team. Your Rouleur can lead for a few turns, absorbing exhaustion while your Sprinteur drafts behind. Then when the Rouleur slows down from exhaustion, your Sprinteur rockets past with a saved nine. Think of your Rouleur as the engine and your Sprinteur as the finisher.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Ignoring Deck Management
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been playing their highest available card every single turn for both cyclists. They burned through their best cards early. Now, with several turns left in the race, Player 2's draws are all threes, fours, and exhaustion twos. They have no burst left when they need it most.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Every card you play is gone forever. There is no reshuffling. If you play all your high cards early, your late-game draws will be terrible. Play medium-value cards to keep pace and save your highest cards for the final sprint. Deck management is the difference between winning and fading in the last stretch.

walkthrough.title: The Final Sprint
walkthrough.text: The finish line is in sight. Player 1 has been saving a nine for their Sprinteur. The pack is bunched up on the straight. Player 1 plays the nine and their Sprinteur rockets forward. Other players play their remaining high cards too, but Player 1 had the better deck management. Their Sprinteur crosses the finish line first!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have just raced your first Flamme Rouge tutorial. You have seen how card selection, slipstreaming, exhaustion, and deck management all connect. Remember: save your sprint nines for the finish, stay in the pack to avoid exhaustion, and manage your deck for the late game. You are ready to race for real!

walkthrough.title: Before You Race
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full race, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common racing mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card values, slipstreaming rules, and hill effects.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your race. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new racers make. First, playing sprint nines on the first few turns and leading the pack into exhaustion. Second, trying to break away mid-race when slipstreaming will catch you. Third, burning all high-value cards early and having nothing left for the finish. Fourth, forgetting to coordinate your two cyclists as a team. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Draft Behind the Pack
walkthrough.text: The single most important tactic in Flamme Rouge is drafting. Stay close to the pack and let slipstreaming give you free movement every turn. Being at the front earns exhaustion cards that permanently weaken your deck. Being in the middle or back of the pack gets you free forward movement without any penalty. Let other players take the lead and suffer the consequences.

walkthrough.title: Save Your Sprint Cards
walkthrough.text: The Sprinteur's three nine-value cards are your most powerful weapons. Do not play them until the finish line is within nine spaces or you are setting up for a final push. Playing a nine mid-race wastes its value because the pack will catch up through slipstreaming and you will eat exhaustion. Save nines for a decisive final sprint when the gap matters.

walkthrough.title: Rouleur as Your Workhorse
walkthrough.text: The Rouleur deck has consistent values from three to seven. Use your Rouleur to maintain steady progress throughout the race. The Rouleur is excellent on hills because the five-cap uphill barely affects them while the Sprinteur's nines are wasted. Let the Rouleur do the work through the middle of the race while saving the Sprinteur for the end.

walkthrough.title: Team Coordination
walkthrough.text: Think of your two cyclists as a team working together. Your Rouleur can lead for a few turns, absorbing exhaustion while your Sprinteur hides behind in the draft. When the Rouleur slows from accumulated exhaustion, your fresh Sprinteur can launch past with saved high cards. Sacrifice one rider's deck quality to preserve the other's finishing power.

walkthrough.title: Hill Strategy
walkthrough.text: Uphill sections cap card values at five and downhill sections set a minimum of five. On uphills, play your lowest cards because anything above five is wasted. Save high cards for flat sections or the finish. On downhills, play your exhaustion twos or lowest cards because they get boosted to five automatically. Hills are where deck management really pays off.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Track
walkthrough.text: Before the race starts, look at the track layout. Identify where the hills are, where narrow sections create bottlenecks, and how far the finish line is from the last hill. Plan your card usage around these features. If there is a long flat stretch before the finish, that is where you want your sprint cards. If there is a hill right before the end, the Rouleur may be your finisher instead.

walkthrough.title: Exhaustion Mitigation
walkthrough.text: Every exhaustion card in your deck is a future turn where you might draw a two instead of something useful. The best mitigation is simply not leading. Stay in the pack. If you must lead, try to lead with your Rouleur rather than your Sprinteur, because the Rouleur's consistent deck handles exhaustion dilution better than the Sprinteur's boom-or-bust deck.

Burning high cards early = nothing left for finish
Forgetting to coordinate two cyclists as a team

walkthrough.title: Peloton Riding
walkthrough.text: The most consistent winning strategy is peloton riding. Stay in the middle of the pack for as long as possible, collecting free slipstream movement every turn while other players absorb exhaustion at the front. The goal is to arrive at the final stretch with a clean deck, full of high-value cards, while opponents are drawing exhaustion twos. Position both cyclists in the pack and only break away in the last two or three turns.

walkthrough.title: The Leadout Train
walkthrough.text: This is the classic cycling team tactic adapted for the game. Position your Rouleur ahead of your Sprinteur. The Rouleur plays high cards to maintain the lead, absorbing all the exhaustion while the Sprinteur drafts behind for free movement. When the finish is close, the Rouleur pulls aside and the Sprinteur plays back-to-back nines to rocket across the line. Your Rouleur's deck gets destroyed, but your Sprinteur's deck stays fresh.

walkthrough.title: Hill Optimization
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their entire card selection around hill placement on the track. On uphills, play your twos, threes, and exhaustion cards because everything caps at five anyway. On downhills, play your remaining low cards because they get boosted to five. Save all your sixes, sevens, and nines for flat sections where their full value matters. This strategy effectively turns bad cards into good ones by playing them at the right terrain.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Strategy
walkthrough.text: Use one cyclist to block opponents at narrow track sections or bottlenecks. If your Rouleur is in front at a narrow section, playing a low card keeps them moving slowly while the pack stacks up behind. This prevents opponents from passing while your Sprinteur sits safely in the draft. Blocking is especially effective on uphill narrow sections where everyone is capped at five anyway.

walkthrough.title: Sprint Timing Calculation
walkthrough.text: Precise sprint timing wins races. Count the spaces to the finish line and calculate exactly when to play your nines. If the finish is 14 spaces away, playing a five this turn and a nine next turn gets you there. Factor in slipstreaming too. If you expect one slipstream move per turn, you effectively gain one extra space. The player who starts their sprint at the mathematically optimal moment will beat players who sprint too early or too late.

walkthrough.title: Deck Counting
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track which cards they have already played and which remain in their deck. If you know you have two nines and three fives left in your Sprinteur deck, you can calculate the probability of drawing them. When your deck is low, you know exactly what is coming. This information lets you plan your final turns with precision rather than hoping for good draws.

walkthrough.title: Forcing Opponents to Lead
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best strategy is to play slightly lower cards to avoid being at the front. If you and an opponent are neck and neck, playing a four instead of a six can keep you behind them. They lead, they get exhaustion, and you get free slipstreaming. This is a psychological game. Experienced players will try to force each other into the lead. The player who is best at staying second wins.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Race Campaign Thinking
walkthrough.text: If your group plays multiple races in a session or uses a campaign mode, think about consistency across races. Avoid strategies that only work on specific track layouts. The peloton riding approach works on almost any track. Save aggressive sprint strategies for tracks with long flat finishes. Adaptability across different track configurations is the mark of an elite Flamme Rouge player.

Break away only in last 2-3 turns with high-value cards
Most consistent winning strategy

entries.definition: One of your two cyclists. Has a deck of 15 cards with consistent values: three sets each of 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7. The steady, reliable rider for mid-race progress.
One of your two cyclists. Has a deck of 15 cards with extreme values: three 2s, three 3s, three 4s, three 5s, and three 9s. The explosive burst rider for the final sprint.
A card with a value of 2 shuffled into a cyclist's deck as a penalty. Gained when a cyclist is at the front of a group with no one ahead after the slipstream phase. Permanently weakens the deck.

After all movement, any cyclist with exactly one empty space between them and the next cyclist ahead moves forward one space for free. Checked repeatedly until no more moves trigger. The key tactical mechanic of the game.
Track section that caps the maximum card value at 5. Even a 9-value card only moves a cyclist 5 spaces uphill. Equalizes all riders and favors the Rouleur's consistent values.
Track section that sets the minimum card value to 5. Even a 2-value exhaustion card moves a cyclist 5 spaces downhill. Ideal for playing low-value and exhaustion cards.
The track is two lanes wide. Two cyclists can occupy the same row side by side. Movement into partially occupied rows slides the cyclist into the empty lane.
A track section that narrows to one lane wide. Creates bottlenecks where cyclists must stack single-file. Strategic for blocking opponents.
Marked on the final track tile. The first cyclist of any player to cross wins the race for their team. If multiple cross on the same turn, furthest past the line wins.
The section at the beginning of the track where all cyclists are placed during setup. Starting positions are chosen by players in turn order.
Move a cyclist forward exactly the number of spaces shown on their played card. Cannot pass through occupied spaces; stack behind the last cyclist in a group.
During the movement phase, the cyclist furthest ahead on the track moves first. This prevents conflicts when multiple cyclists are close together.
Decks are never reshuffled in Flamme Rouge. When your deck runs out, draw from whatever cards remain. Every card played is gone permanently. Exhaustion cards stay in the deck forever.
Each turn, draw 4 cards from each cyclist's deck. Choose 1 to play, shuffle the other 3 back. If fewer than 4 cards remain, draw what you can.
The main group of cyclists riding together. Staying in the peloton provides slipstream benefits and avoids exhaustion. Breaking away from the peloton is risky.
When a cyclist pulls ahead of the peloton with a gap. The breakaway cyclist leads alone, gaining exhaustion each turn. Only effective near the finish line.
A team tactic where one cyclist leads to absorb exhaustion while the other drafts behind. Near the finish, the leading cyclist pulls aside and the trailing cyclist sprints past.
The strategy of choosing when to play high vs. low cards throughout the race. Saving high cards for the finish and playing low cards on hills is key to winning.
Cardboard pieces including straights, curves, and hills that connect together to form the race course. Different configurations create different racing experiences.
All players draw, choose, and reveal cards at the same time. No waiting for other players' turns. Keeps the game fast and prevents analysis paralysis.
